subject: Mathematics
text: A real valued function is continuous at a point in its domain if the limit of the function at that point equals the value of the function at that point. A function is continuous if it is continuous on the whole of its domain. A function f(x) is said to be continuous at x = c if {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow c^{-}}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow c^{+}}{/tex} f(x) = f(c)
text_html:
A real valued function is continuous at a point in its domain if the limit of the function at that point equals the value of the function at that point. A function is continuous if it is continuous on the whole of its domain.
A function f(x) is said to be continuous at x = c if {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow c^{-}}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow c^{+}}{/tex} f(x) = f(c)

subject: Mathematics
text: Sum, difference, product and quotient of continuous functions are continuous. i.e., If f and g are continuous functions, then (f {tex}\pm{/tex} g) (x) = f(x) {tex}\pm{/tex} g(x) is continuous. (f {tex}\cdot{/tex} g) (x) = f(x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} g (x) is continuous. {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{g}}\right)(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})}{/tex} (wherever g(x) {tex}\neq{/tex} 0) is continuous.
text_html:
Sum, difference, product and quotient of continuous functions are continuous. i.e., If f and g are continuous functions, then

  1. (f {tex}\pm{/tex} g) (x) = f(x) {tex}\pm{/tex} g(x) is continuous.
  2. (f {tex}\cdot{/tex} g) (x) = f(x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} g (x) is continuous.
  3. {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{g}}\right)(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})}{/tex} (wherever g(x) {tex}\neq{/tex} 0) is continuous.

subject: Mathematics
text: Modulus function is continuous on R. Trigonometric functions are continuous in their respective domains. Exponential function is continuous on R. Every polynomial function is continuous on R. Greatest integer function is continuous on non-integral real numbers.
text_html:
• Modulus function is continuous on R.
  • Trigonometric functions are continuous in their respective domains.
  • Exponential function is continuous on R.
  • Every polynomial function is continuous on R.
  • Greatest integer function is continuous on non-integral real numbers.

subject: Mathematics
text: Implicit function is a function with multiple variables, and one of the variables is a function of the other set of variables. A function f(x, y) = 0 such that it is a function of x, y, expressed as an equation with the variables on one side, and equalized to zero.
text_html: Implicit function is a function with multiple variables, and one of the variables is a function of the other set of variables. A function f(x, y) = 0 such that it is a function of x, y, expressed as an equation with the variables on one side, and equalized to zero.

subject: Mathematics
text: In implicit differentiation, we differentiate each side of an equation with two variables (usually x and y) by treating one of the variables as a function of the other. This calls for using the chain rule.
text_html: In implicit differentiation, we differentiate each side of an equation with two variables (usually x and y) by treating one of the variables as a function of the other. This calls for using the chain rule.

subject: Mathematics
text: Logarithmic differentiation is a powerful technique to differentiate functions of the form f(x) = [u (x)] v(x) . Here both f(x) and u(x) need to be positive for this technique to make sense
text_html: Logarithmic differentiation is a powerful technique to differentiate functions of the form f(x) = [u (x)]v(x). Here both f(x) and u(x) need to be positive for this technique to make sense

subject: Mathematics
text: Steps for logarithmic differentiation Take the natural log of both sides. Use log properties to simplify the equations. Differentiate both sides using implicit differentiation and other derivative rules. Solve for {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} . Replace y with f(x).
text_html:
Steps for logarithmic differentiation

  1. Take the natural log of both sides.
  2. Use log properties to simplify the equations.
  3. Differentiate both sides using implicit differentiation and other derivative rules.
  4. Solve for {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.
  5. Replace y with f(x).

subject: Mathematics
topic: Derivatives of Functions in Parametric Forms (XII-5.6)
text: A parametric derivative is a derivative of a dependent variable with respect to another dependent variable that is taken when both variables depend on an independent third variable. The third variable is called as parameter.
text_html: A parametric derivative is a derivative of a dependent variable with respect to another dependent variable that is taken when both variables depend on an independent third variable. The third variable is called as parameter.

subject: Mathematics
topic: Derivatives of Functions in Parametric Forms (XII-5.6)
text: If x = f(t), y = g(t) is said to be parametric form with t as a parameter then {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}{/tex}
text_html: If x = f(t), y = g(t) is said to be parametric form with t as a parameter then {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: The Second Order Derivative is defined as the derivative of the first derivative of the given function. If y = f(x) then second derivative is denoted by {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}{/tex} or {tex}f^{\prime}{/tex} (x) or y 2 or {tex}y^{\prime}{/tex} .
text_html: The Second Order Derivative is defined as the derivative of the first derivative of the given function. If y = f(x) then second derivative is denoted by {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}{/tex} or {tex}f^{\prime}{/tex}(x) or y2 or {tex}y^{\prime}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
text: The second derivative of a given function corresponds to the curvature or concavity of the graph. If the second-order derivative value is positive, then the graph of a function is upwardly concave. If the second-order derivative value is negative, then the graph of a function is downwardly open.
text_html: The second derivative of a given function corresponds to the curvature or concavity of the graph. If the second-order derivative value is positive, then the graph of a function is upwardly concave. If the second-order derivative value is negative, then the graph of a function is downwardly open.
